subject: Science

unit.unitTitle: Humans and Animals: Your Amazing Body Systems
unit.unitOverview: Right now, without you thinking about it even once, your heart is squeezing and pushing blood around your body. Your lungs are pulling in fresh air. Your stomach may be quietly breaking down the bariis and fish you ate at lunch. And your brain is reading these very words and turning them into ideas. Your body is the most wonderful machine you will ever own, and in this unit you are going to open it up with your mind and discover how it works.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 6 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name and locate the main organs of the body: heart, lungs, stomach, intestines, kidneys, and brain.
Describe how the circulatory system uses the heart, blood, and blood vessels to carry oxygen and nutrients around the body.
Explain how the respiratory system breathes air in and out and exchanges gases in the lungs.
Trace the journey of food through the digestive system and explain how nutrients reach your cells.
Measure your own pulse rate and breathing rate correctly, and record the numbers in a clear table.
Predict and explain how exercise changes your heart rate and breathing rate, using scientific words.
Describe what a healthy, balanced diet looks like using foods from your own home and region.
Explain how the body systems work together, and how smoking, khat, and other harmful substances damage them.

Here are the important words for this unit. Do not try to memorise them all at once. Meet them here, then use them again and again as you read, and they will become part of how you think and speak about your body.
A body part made of tissues that does a special job.

concepts.title: Organs, Systems, and Teamwork
concepts.explanation: Imagine a busy town. No single person can run the whole town alone. The baker bakes, the water carrier brings water, the builder builds, the doctor heals. Each person has a job, and together they keep the town alive. Your body works in exactly the same way. Each organ is like a worker with one special job, and organs group together into systems, which are like the different services that keep the town running. Let us picture the inside of the body. High up and protected inside the strong cage of your ribs sit the…
concepts.example: Notice something clever: the body protects its most important organs. The skull is a helmet of bone around the brain. The ribcage is a curved shield around the heart and lungs. This is not an accident. The organs that…

concepts.title: The Circulatory System - Your Body's River
concepts.explanation: The circulatory system is made of three main parts: the heart (the pump), the blood (the delivery liquid), and the blood vessels (the pipes). Together they form a river system that reaches every single part of you, from the top of your head to the tip of your smallest toe. Think of the heart as a strong, tireless pump. It is made almost entirely of muscle, and it squeezes and relaxes about 70 to 100 times every minute when you are resting. It never takes a holiday. While you sleep, while you eat your canjeero at…
concepts.example: The heart is cleverly divided into two sides, a right side and a left side. This is one of the most important ideas in the whole unit, so read slowly. The right side collects blood that has returned from the body, tired…

concepts.title: Feeling the Beat - Pulse and Heart Rate
concepts.explanation: You cannot see your heart, but you can absolutely feel that it is working. Every time the heart squeezes, it sends a little pressure wave rushing through your arteries. Where an artery runs close to the skin, you can feel that wave as a gentle throb. This throb is your pulse, and the number of pulses in one minute is your heart rate, measured in beats per minute (bpm). To find your pulse, turn one hand palm-up. Place the tips of your first two fingers on the wrist, just below the base of your thumb, and press very…
concepts.example: A resting child of your age usually has a heart rate somewhere around 70 to 100 beats per minute, though everybody is a little different, and that is perfectly normal. The best way to measure it is to count the beats…

concepts.title: Exercise and the Working Heart
concepts.explanation: Here is something you can prove on your own body today. When you run, jump, or play, your heart rate shoots up. Why? When your muscles work hard, they burn through oxygen and food much faster than when you are resting. Your muscles cry out, in effect, 'We need more oxygen, quickly!' Your heart answers by beating faster and harder, so that oxygen-rich blood is delivered to your muscles at a much faster rate. At the same time you breathe faster, to load more oxygen into that blood. When Hassan sprints across the…
concepts.example: This is one of the biggest reasons that staying active matters so much. Every time you run, skip, swim in the Indian Ocean, help carry water, or play a fast game, you are giving your heart a workout. Just as your arm…

concepts.title: The Respiratory System - The Breath of Life
concepts.explanation: Put one hand on your chest and take a slow, deep breath in. Feel your chest rise. Now let it out and feel it fall. You have just watched your respiratory system at work, and you do this around 12 to 20 times every minute of your life, awake or asleep, without ever having to remember to do it. Air enters through your nose or mouth. Inside the nose it is warmed, moistened, and cleaned by tiny hairs and sticky mucus that trap dust. From there the air travels down your windpipe, a firm tube in the front of your neck.…
concepts.example: These tiny air sacs are where the real magic happens, in a process called gas exchange. Each air sac is wrapped in the thinnest of blood vessels. When fresh air fills the sac, oxygen slips out of the air, through the…

concepts.title: The Digestive System - From Food to Fuel
concepts.explanation: When you eat a plate of bariis with fish and vegetables, that food cannot be used by your body straight away. It is far too big. Your cells are microscopically tiny, and they can only take in nutrients that have been broken down into extremely small pieces. The job of the digestive system is to break your food down, step by step, until it is small enough to be absorbed and carried to your cells. Digestion is really a long journey down a tube that runs all the way from your mouth to the end of your gut. The journey…
concepts.example: When you swallow, the soft ball of food slides down a muscular tube into your stomach, a stretchy bag on the left side of your belly. The stomach is like a strong mixing pot. Its muscular walls churn the food, and it…

explorations.title: Finding and Counting Your Pulse
explorations.context: To locate your own pulse and measure your resting heart rate accurately. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: how many times do you think your heart beats in one minute while you are sitting still? Write your prediction so you can…
explorations.prompt: How close was your real resting heart rate to your prediction?
explorations.answer: How close was your real resting heart rate to your prediction? Were your three readings exactly the same, or a little different? Why is it a good idea to take an average? Is your resting heart rate inside the normal range of about 70 to 100 bpm for a child?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To locate your own pulse and measure your resting heart rate accurately. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: how many times do you think your heart beats in one minute while you are sitting still? Write your prediction so you can…

explorations.title: How Exercise Changes Your Heart Rate
explorations.context: To find out how exercise affects your heart rate, and how long it takes to return to normal afterwards.
explorations.prompt: Complete this sentence before you begin: 'I think that after exercise my heart rate will ___ because...' A hypothesis is your best scientific guess with a reason.
explorations.answer: By how much did your heart rate increase (after exercise minus resting)? Was your hypothesis correct? How many minutes did it take for your heart rate to return close to resting? This is your recovery time. Explain, using the words oxygen and muscles, why your heart beat faster during exercise.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how exercise affects your heart rate, and how long it takes to return to normal afterwards.

explorations.title: Counting Your Breathing Rate
explorations.context: To measure how many breaths you take each minute at rest and after activity, and to see how breathing links to heart rate. Make a Prediction How many breaths do you think you take in one minute while resting? And do you think this will change after exercise?…
explorations.prompt: Did your breathing rate go up after exercise? By how much?
explorations.answer: Did your breathing rate go up after exercise? By how much? Why does your body need you to breathe faster when you exercise? Use the word oxygen in your answer. How are breathing and heartbeat connected? Explain how the respiratory and circulatory systems worked as a team during your exercise.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure how many breaths you take each minute at rest and after activity, and to see how breathing links to heart rate. Make a Prediction How many breaths do you think you take in one minute while resting? And do you think this will change after exercise?…

explorations.title: A Balloon Model of the Lungs
explorations.context: To build a simple model that shows how your lungs fill with air and empty again. Make a Prediction When you blow into a balloon, it gets bigger. Predict what this tells you about what happens to your lungs when you breathe in.
explorations.prompt: Which breath filled the balloon more, the normal one or the deep one? What does this tell you about how much air your lungs can hold?
explorations.answer: Which breath filled the balloon more, the normal one or the deep one? What does this tell you about how much air your lungs can hold? The balloon getting bigger is like what happening inside your chest when you breathe in? Why might a person who exercises a lot, like a swimmer, be able to fill the…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a simple model that shows how your lungs fill with air and empty again. Make a Prediction When you blow into a balloon, it gets bigger. Predict what this tells you about what happens to your lungs when you breathe in.

explorations.title: Modelling Digestion in a Bag
explorations.context: To model how the stomach mashes food, and to see why chewing helps digestion. This is a model only; no real digestion or eating of the mixture happens. Make a Prediction Predict which will break up faster inside the 'stomach' bag: a whole piece of bread or a…
explorations.prompt: Which bag broke down more easily, and was your prediction correct?
explorations.answer: Which bag broke down more easily, and was your prediction correct? In this model, what did your squeezing hands stand for? What did the crumbling stand for? Using your result, explain why chewing your food well helps your real stomach and intestines do their job.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how the stomach mashes food, and to see why chewing helps digestion. This is a model only; no real digestion or eating of the mixture happens. Make a Prediction Predict which will break up faster inside the 'stomach' bag: a whole piece of bread or a…

methods.title: Finding and Counting Your Pulse
methods.example: To locate your own pulse and measure your resting heart rate accurately. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: how many times do you think your heart beats in one minute while you are sitting still? Write your prediction so you can…
steps: Sit still and calm for two minutes so your heart is at its resting rate.
Turn one hand palm-up. Place the tips of your first two fingers (not your thumb) on your wrist, just below the base of the thumb.
Press very gently and move your fingers a little until you feel a soft, steady tapping.
When you are ready, count every beat for a full 60 seconds using your clock.
Write the number down. This is your resting heart rate in beats per minute.

methods.title: How Exercise Changes Your Heart Rate
methods.example: To find out how exercise affects your heart rate, and how long it takes to return to normal afterwards.
steps: Sit still for two minutes, then measure your resting heart rate for 60 seconds. Record it.
Now do a safe exercise for one minute, such as star jumps, jogging on the spot, or stepping up and down on a low, stable step. Keep a steady pace.
The moment you stop, measure your heart rate for 60 seconds and record it.
Sit down and rest. After one minute of resting, measure again and record.
Keep measuring once every minute until your heart rate is back near your resting number. Record each reading.

methods.title: Counting Your Breathing Rate
methods.example: To measure how many breaths you take each minute at rest and after activity, and to see how breathing links to heart rate. Make a Prediction How many breaths do you think you take in one minute while resting? And do you think this will change after exercise?…
steps: Sit still and breathe normally. Do not try to control your breathing, just let it happen.
Count one full breath as one breath in and one breath out together.
Count how many breaths you take in 60 seconds at rest. Record it.
Now do one minute of safe exercise, such as jogging on the spot.
Straight after, count your breaths for 60 seconds again and record it.

methods.title: A Balloon Model of the Lungs
methods.example: To build a simple model that shows how your lungs fill with air and empty again. Make a Prediction When you blow into a balloon, it gets bigger. Predict what this tells you about what happens to your lungs when you breathe in.
steps: Take a normal breath, then blow it out gently into a balloon in one single breath. Pinch the neck closed.
Measure around the widest part of the balloon with the tape (or wrap string around it, then measure the string). Record the size.
Let the air out. Rest a moment.
Now take the deepest breath you can, and blow it all out into the balloon in one breath. Measure and record again.
Compare the two balloon sizes.

methods.title: Modelling Digestion in a Bag
methods.example: To model how the stomach mashes food, and to see why chewing helps digestion. This is a model only; no real digestion or eating of the mixture happens. Make a Prediction Predict which will break up faster inside the 'stomach' bag: a whole piece of bread or a…
steps: Into bag 1, put one whole piece of bread. Into bag 2, put an equal piece that you have first torn into small crumbs (this stands for chewing).
Add the same small amount of warm water to each bag. Seal both bags well.
Gently squeeze and squash each bag with your hands for two minutes. Your hands are acting like the muscular walls of the stomach.
Look at the mixture inside each bag. Which one has turned into a smoother, more even mush?
Wash your hands well afterwards and throw the bags away. Do not eat the mixture.

workedExamples.title: Doing the Maths of Your Body
workedExamples.prompt: Science often uses numbers, and your body gives you numbers you can measure yourself. Let us work through some examples carefully, showing every step, so that you can do the same with your own results.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Turning a 15-second count into a heart rate Counting your pulse for a full minute is accurate but slow. A faster way is to count for 15 seconds and then multiply, because there are four lots of 15 seconds in one minute. Suppose Idris counts 19 beats in 15 seconds. What is his heart rate in beats per minute? Write down what you counted: 19 beats in 15 seconds. Ask: how many 15-second blocks are in one minute? Answer: 60 divided by 15 = 4 blocks.

workedExamples.title: 1: Turning a 15-second count into a heart rate
workedExamples.prompt: Counting your pulse for a full minute is accurate but slow. A faster way is to count for 15 seconds and then multiply, because there are four lots of 15 seconds in one minute. Suppose Idris counts 19 beats in 15 seconds. What is his heart rate in beats per…
workedExamples.solution: Write down what you counted: 19 beats in 15 seconds. Ask: how many 15-second blocks are in one minute? Answer: 60 divided by 15 = 4 blocks. Multiply your count by 4: 19 x 4 = 76. Write the answer with its unit: Idris's heart rate is 76 beats per minute (bpm).

workedExamples.title: 2: Finding the average of several readings
workedExamples.prompt: Scientists take a measurement more than once and find the average, because this smooths out little errors. Halima measured her resting pulse three times and got 74, 78, and 76 bpm. What is her average resting heart rate?
workedExamples.solution: Add all the readings together: 74 + 78 + 76 = 228. Count how many readings there are: 3 readings. Divide the total by the number of readings: 228 divided by 3 = 76. Write the answer: Halima's average resting heart rate is 76 bpm.

workedExamples.title: 3: Comparing rest and exercise
workedExamples.prompt: Omar's heart rate was 80 bpm resting. Straight after doing star jumps it was 132 bpm. By how much did his heart rate increase?
workedExamples.solution: Write both numbers: resting = 80 bpm, after exercise = 132 bpm. To find the increase, subtract the smaller from the larger: 132 - 80 = 52. Write the answer: Omar's heart rate increased by 52 bpm because of the exercise. This large jump makes perfect sense: Omar's working muscles needed far more oxygen, so his heart sped up to deliver it. The numbers tell the same story as the science.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: During exercise, your heart rate: (a) goes down (b) stays the same (c) goes up (d) stops
workedExamples.solution: 5 (c) goes up Working muscles need more oxygen, so the heart beats faster.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Most nutrients from food are absorbed in the: (a) mouth (b) stomach (c) small intestine (d) large intestine
workedExamples.solution: 6 (c) small intestine The stomach begins digestion, but the small intestine absorbs most nutrients.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which food is the best source of protein for growth? (a) sugar (b) grilled fish (c) plain rice (d) sweets
workedExamples.solution: 7 (b) grilled fish Fish is rich in protein, which the body uses to grow and repair.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe is the: (a) diaphragm (b) heart (c) kidney (d) rib
workedExamples.solution: 8 (a) diaphragm This dome-shaped muscle pulls down to draw air in.

practice.prompt: Which organ pumps blood around the body? (a) lungs (b) heart (c) stomach (d) brain
practice.answer: 1 (b) heart The heart is the muscular pump; the lungs handle air, not pumping blood.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart are called: (a) veins (b) nerves (c) arteries (d) intestines
practice.answer: 2 (c) arteries Arteries carry blood Away from the heart; veins carry it back.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide happens in the: (a) stomach (b) heart (c) lungs (d) kidneys
practice.answer: 3 (c) lungs Gas exchange happens in the tiny air sacs of the lungs.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: You should feel your pulse using your: (a) thumb (b) first two fingers (c) whole palm (d) elbow
practice.answer: 4 (b) first two fingers The thumb has its own faint pulse, which confuses the count.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: During exercise, your heart rate: (a) goes down (b) stays the same (c) goes up (d) stops
practice.answer: 5 (c) goes up Working muscles need more oxygen, so the heart beats faster.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Most nutrients from food are absorbed in the: (a) mouth (b) stomach (c) small intestine (d) large intestine
practice.answer: 6 (c) small intestine The stomach begins digestion, but the small intestine absorbs most nutrients.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which food is the best source of protein for growth? (a) sugar (b) grilled fish (c) plain rice (d) sweets
practice.answer: 7 (b) grilled fish Fish is rich in protein, which the body uses to grow and repair.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe is the: (a) diaphragm (b) heart (c) kidney (d) rib
practice.answer: 8 (a) diaphragm This dome-shaped muscle pulls down to draw air in.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these harms the tiny air sacs in the lungs? (a) drinking water (b) cigarette smoke (c) eating dates (d) sleeping well
practice.answer: 9 (b) cigarette smoke Tar in smoke clogs and damages the air sacs, reducing oxygen uptake.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A normal resting heart rate for a child is about: (a) 5 to 10 bpm (b) 20 to 30 bpm (c) 70 to 100 bpm (d) 200 to 300 bpm
practice.answer: 10 (c) 70 to 100 bpm This is the normal resting range for a child.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which body structure protects the heart and lungs? (a) the skull (b) the ribcage (c) the hip bones (d) the jaw
practice.answer: 11 (b) the ribcage The curved ribs form a shield around the heart and lungs.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The right side of the heart pumps blood to the: (a) legs (b) brain (c) lungs (d) stomach
practice.answer: 12 (c) lungs The right side sends blood to the lungs to collect oxygen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Finding and Counting Your Pulse
activities.materials: A clock, watch, or phone that shows seconds; A pencil and your notebook; A quiet place to sit
steps: Sit still and calm for two minutes so your heart is at its resting rate.
Turn one hand palm-up. Place the tips of your first two fingers (not your thumb) on your wrist, just below the base of the thumb.
Press very gently and move your fingers a little until you feel a soft, steady tapping.
When you are ready, count every beat for a full 60 seconds using your clock.
Write the number down. This is your resting heart rate in beats per minute.

activities.title: How Exercise Changes Your Heart Rate
activities.materials: A clock or phone timer; Your notebook and pencil; A clear, safe space to move; Water to drink afterwards
steps: Sit still for two minutes, then measure your resting heart rate for 60 seconds. Record it.
Now do a safe exercise for one minute, such as star jumps, jogging on the spot, or stepping up and down on a low, stable step. Keep a steady pace.
The moment you stop, measure your heart rate for 60 seconds and record it.
Sit down and rest. After one minute of resting, measure again and record.
Keep measuring once every minute until your heart rate is back near your resting number. Record each reading.

activities.title: Counting Your Breathing Rate
activities.materials: A clock or timer; Your notebook and pencil
steps: Sit still and breathe normally. Do not try to control your breathing, just let it happen.
Count one full breath as one breath in and one breath out together.
Count how many breaths you take in 60 seconds at rest. Record it.
Now do one minute of safe exercise, such as jogging on the spot.
Straight after, count your breaths for 60 seconds again and record it.

activities.title: A Balloon Model of the Lungs
activities.materials: One or two clean balloons; A measuring tape or a piece of string and a ruler; Your notebook
steps: Take a normal breath, then blow it out gently into a balloon in one single breath. Pinch the neck closed.
Measure around the widest part of the balloon with the tape (or wrap string around it, then measure the string). Record the size.
Let the air out. Rest a moment.
Now take the deepest breath you can, and blow it all out into the balloon in one breath. Measure and record again.
Compare the two balloon sizes.

activities.title: Modelling Digestion in a Bag
activities.materials: Two strong, sealable plastic food bags (or two bags you can hold shut tightly); Two equal pieces of soft bread or a soft biscuit; A little warm water; Your hands and your notebook
steps: Into bag 1, put one whole piece of bread. Into bag 2, put an equal piece that you have first torn into small crumbs (this stands for chewing).
Add the same small amount of warm water to each bag. Seal both bags well.
Gently squeeze and squash each bag with your hands for two minutes. Your hands are acting like the muscular walls of the stomach.
Look at the mixture inside each bag. Which one has turned into a smoother, more even mush?
Wash your hands well afterwards and throw the bags away. Do not eat the mixture.

activities.title: Observe: The Digestive System - From Food to Fuel
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the digestive system - from food to fuel around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: A body part that does a special job, made of tissues.
A group of organs working together on one big task.
Heart, blood, and blood vessels; moves blood around the body.
The muscular pump that keeps blood moving; about the size of your fist.
Red liquid that carries oxygen, nutrients, and waste.
A vessel carrying blood away from the heart; thick walls.
A vessel carrying blood back to the heart; thinner walls, has valves.
The beat felt in an artery as the heart pushes blood; used to find heart rate.
Nose, windpipe, and lungs; breathes air in and out.
Two spongy organs where gas exchange happens.
The dome-shaped muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe.

vocabulary.meaning: A body part that does a special job, made of tissues.
vocabulary.example: The muscular organ that pumps blood without ever resting.

vocabulary.meaning: A group of organs working together on one big task.
Heart, blood, and blood vessels; moves blood around the body.
vocabulary.example: The circulatory system delivers oxygen and food to every corner of your body.

vocabulary.meaning: The muscular pump that keeps blood moving; about the size of your fist.
vocabulary.example: Right now, without you thinking about it even once, your heart is squeezing and pushing blood around your body.

vocabulary.meaning: Red liquid that carries oxygen, nutrients, and waste.
vocabulary.example: Right now, without you thinking about it even once, your heart is squeezing and pushing blood around your body.

vocabulary.meaning: A vessel carrying blood away from the heart; thick walls.
vocabulary.example: You feel your pulse in an artery at your wrist.

vocabulary.meaning: A vessel carrying blood back to the heart; thinner walls, has valves.
vocabulary.example: Practise saying artery, vein, diaphragm, and gas exchange until they feel natural in your mouth.

vocabulary.meaning: The beat felt in an artery as the heart pushes blood; used to find heart rate.
vocabulary.example: You will feel your pulse, count your breaths, and watch how your body answers when you move.
Predict and explain how exercise changes your heart rate and breathing rate, using scientific words.

vocabulary.meaning: Nose, windpipe, and lungs; breathes air in and out.
vocabulary.example: The respiratory system brings in the air you need and removes the waste gas you do not.

vocabulary.meaning: Two spongy organs where gas exchange happens.
vocabulary.example: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Ask: 'Can you give me a quick tour of the main organs inside my body and where each one sits?' Then ask your tutor to quiz you on where the heart, lungs, and stomach are.

vocabulary.meaning: The dome-shaped muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe.
vocabulary.example: Below your lungs is a large dome-shaped muscle called the diaphragm.

commonMistakes: Saying the heart is only on the far left.
The whole heart sits mostly in the middle.
Saying food is fully digested in the stomach.
The stomach only starts the job.
Using the thumb to take a pulse.
The thumb has its own pulse.
Thinking only unfit people need to protect their hearts.

connections.text: Next unit (Living Things in the Environment): the idea of parts working together as a team appears again in food chains and ecosystems.
the food you digest links to how energy flows from the sun to plants to animals.
counting beats, finding averages, and drawing graphs use your number skills.
everything here connects to real choices about food, exercise, cleanliness, and avoiding harmful substances.
Compare heart rates across animals: why does a camel's heart beat slower than a mouse's?
Find out what athletes do to make their hearts and lungs stronger, and try some safe versions yourself.

fluency.answer: 1 (b) heart The heart is the muscular pump; the lungs handle air, not pumping blood.

fluency.prompt: You should feel your pulse using your: (a) thumb (b) first two fingers (c) whole palm (d) elbow
fluency.answer: 4 (b) first two fingers The thumb has its own faint pulse, which confuses the count.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 4 (b) first two fingers The thumb has its own faint pulse, which confuses the count.

fluency.prompt: During exercise, your heart rate: (a) goes down (b) stays the same (c) goes up (d) stops
fluency.answer: 5 (c) goes up Working muscles need more oxygen, so the heart beats faster.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 5 (c) goes up Working muscles need more oxygen, so the heart beats faster.

fluency.prompt: Most nutrients from food are absorbed in the: (a) mouth (b) stomach (c) small intestine (d) large intestine
fluency.answer: 6 (c) small intestine The stomach begins digestion, but the small intestine absorbs most nutrients.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 6 (c) small intestine The stomach begins digestion, but the small intestine absorbs most nutrients.

fluency.prompt: Which food is the best source of protein for growth? (a) sugar (b) grilled fish (c) plain rice (d) sweets
fluency.answer: 7 (b) grilled fish Fish is rich in protein, which the body uses to grow and repair.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 7 (b) grilled fish Fish is rich in protein, which the body uses to grow and repair.

fluency.prompt: The muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe is the: (a) diaphragm (b) heart (c) kidney (d) rib
fluency.answer: 8 (a) diaphragm This dome-shaped muscle pulls down to draw air in.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8 (a) diaphragm This dome-shaped muscle pulls down to draw air in.

fluency.prompt: Which of these harms the tiny air sacs in the lungs? (a) drinking water (b) cigarette smoke (c) eating dates (d) sleeping well
fluency.answer: 9 (b) cigarette smoke Tar in smoke clogs and damages the air sacs, reducing oxygen uptake.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9 (b) cigarette smoke Tar in smoke clogs and damages the air sacs, reducing oxygen uptake.

fluency.prompt: A normal resting heart rate for a child is about: (a) 5 to 10 bpm (b) 20 to 30 bpm (c) 70 to 100 bpm (d) 200 to 300 bpm
fluency.answer: 10 (c) 70 to 100 bpm This is the normal resting range for a child.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (c) 70 to 100 bpm This is the normal resting range for a child.

fluency.prompt: Which body structure protects the heart and lungs? (a) the skull (b) the ribcage (c) the hip bones (d) the jaw
fluency.answer: 11 (b) the ribcage The curved ribs form a shield around the heart and lungs.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (b) the ribcage The curved ribs form a shield around the heart and lungs.

fluency.prompt: The right side of the heart pumps blood to the: (a) legs (b) brain (c) lungs (d) stomach
fluency.answer: 12 (c) lungs The right side sends blood to the lungs to collect oxygen.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (c) lungs The right side sends blood to the lungs to collect oxygen.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to solve these real-life problems.
realProblems.answer: 1 20 beats in 15 seconds. There are 4 lots of 15 seconds in a minute, so 20 x 4 = 80 bpm. Farah's heart rate is 80 beats per minute.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 20 beats in 15 seconds. There are 4 lots of 15 seconds in a minute, so 20 x 4 = 80 bpm. Farah's heart rate is 80 beats per minute.

realProblems.prompt: Farah counts 20 beats in 15 seconds. What is her heart rate in beats per minute? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 2 Add: 76 + 80 + 78 = 234. Divide by 3 readings: 234 / 3 = 78 bpm. Noor's average resting heart rate is 78 bpm. Averaging reduces the effect of small errors.

realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 Add: 76 + 80 + 78 = 234. Divide by 3 readings: 234 / 3 = 78 bpm. Noor's average resting heart rate is 78 bpm. Averaging reduces the effect of small errors.

realProblems.prompt: Noor measured her resting pulse three times: 76, 80, and 78 bpm. What is her average resting heart rate?
realProblems.answer: 3 138 - 84 = 54 bpm increase. It increased because his leg muscles needed more oxygen during football, so his heart pumped faster to deliver it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 138 - 84 = 54 bpm increase. It increased because his leg muscles needed more oxygen during football, so his heart pumped faster to deliver it.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf's heart rate was 84 bpm at rest and 138 bpm right after playing football. By how much did it increase, and why did it increase?
realProblems.answer: 4 Answers vary. A good example: rice (energy), grilled fish or beans (growth), and vegetables and a mango or dates (protection and vitamins), with clean water. A balanced plate has all three parts.

realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 Answers vary. A good example: rice (energy), grilled fish or beans (growth), and vegetables and a mango or dates (protection and vitamins), with clean water. A balanced plate has all three parts.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra's family is planning a healthy dinner. Suggest a balanced meal using foods from your region, and say which part gives energy, which helps growth, and which protects health.
realProblems.answer: 5 They can boil the water before drinking it, which kills germs. This protects the digestive system from disease. Covering and storing it cleanly helps too.

realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 They can boil the water before drinking it, which kills germs. This protects the digestive system from disease. Covering and storing it cleanly helps too.

realProblems.prompt: During a dry season, a family in Garissa is unsure whether their well water is safe. What simple step can they take before drinking it, and which body system does this protect?

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three main parts of the circulatory system.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 The heart, the blood, and the blood vessels (arteries and veins).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between an artery and a vein.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 An artery carries blood away from the heart and has thick, muscular walls; a vein carries blood back to the heart, has thinner walls, and often has valves.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does blood travel to the lungs before going to the rest of the body?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Because the blood returning from the body is low on oxygen, so it must go to the lungs first to collect fresh oxygen before being pumped around the body.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is gas exchange, and where in the body does it happen?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 Gas exchange is oxygen moving from the air into the blood while carbon dioxide moves out of the blood into the air. It happens in the tiny air sacs of the lungs.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe what happens to food in the stomach.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 The stomach churns the food with its muscular walls and adds acid and chemicals that break it down further and kill many germs, turning it into a thick liquid.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two reasons why regular exercise is good for your heart.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 Any two: exercise makes the heart muscle stronger; it helps the heart pump more blood with each beat; it improves recovery time; it lowers the risk of heart disease.

selfAssessment: I can name and locate the main organs of the body: heart, lungs, stomach, intestines, kidneys, and brain.
I can describe how the circulatory system uses the heart, blood, and blood vessels to carry oxygen and nutrients around the body.
I can explain how the respiratory system breathes air in and out and exchanges gases in the lungs.
I can trace the journey of food through the digestive system and explain how nutrients reach your cells.
I can measure your own pulse rate and breathing rate correctly, and record the numbers in a clear table.
I can predict and explain how exercise changes your heart rate and breathing rate, using scientific words.
I can describe what a healthy, balanced diet looks like using foods from your own home and region.
I can explain how the body systems work together, and how smoking, khat, and other harmful substances damage them.
